Cats have impaired UGT1A6 (pseudogene), but anthocyanins exert antioxidant effects in the GI lumen independent of hepatic conjugation, so at typical pet food inclusion levels this is not a clinical concern for cats.
citations_display: Dunlap KL et al., Comp Biochem Physiol A 2006;143:429-434; Court MH & Greenblatt DJ, Pharmacogenetics 2000;10:355-369
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Higher on the label = more benefit.

definition: Fruit containing A-type proanthocyanidins (PACs) that inhibit P-fimbriated E. coli adhesion to uroepithelial cells. Also contains organic acids, flavonoids, and notable oxalate content.
tldr: Contains proanthocyanidins that may reduce bacterial adhesion in the urinary tract. The anti-adhesion mechanism is species-independent. However, cranberries are high in oxalate and vitamin C (an oxalate precursor) — contraindicated in cats predisposed to calcium oxalate urolithiasis.
detail_body: Cranberries contain A-type proanthocyanidins (PACs) that reduce bacterial adhesion in the urinary tract via anti-adhesion mechanisms. In vitro studies confirm PAC-mediated inhibition of E. coli adherence to canine kidney (MDCK) cells. The anti-adhesion mechanism is species-independent. Dogs tolerate cranberry at standard inclusion levels without oxalate concerns in most individuals. For cats, PAC anti-adhesion mechanisms are theoretically applicable, but cranberry is high in oxalate and vitamin C (a metabolic oxalate precursor), and supplementation increases urinary oxalate excretion. Contraindicated in cats with history of or predisposition to calcium oxalate urolithiasis (CaOx accounts for ~40-50% of feline uroliths).
citations_display: Chou HI et al., Am J Vet Res 2016;77:421-427; Bartges JW, J Feline Med Surg 2016;18:712-722
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Higher on the label = more benefit.

definition: Liquid sweetener/humectant produced by enzymatic or acid hydrolysis of corn starch, yielding glucose, maltose, and higher saccharides. Functions as sweetener, humectant, texture agent, and browning contributor.
tldr: Same concerns as sugar with a potentially higher glycemic impact — corn syrup's glucose is more rapidly absorbed than sucrose. Dogs have functional sweet taste receptors, so corn syrup enhances palatability. Cats cannot taste sweetness (Tas1R2 pseudogene), making corn syrup essentially pre-digested, rapidly absorbed glucose entering the bloodstream of an obligate carnivore metabolically unable to efficiently process it.
detail_body: AAFCO defines corn syrup as "concentrated juice derived from corn." GRAS for food use. Commonly used in semi-moist pet foods. Glucose from corn syrup is more rapidly absorbed than sucrose, potentially causing higher glycemic impact. For dogs, same obesity, dental disease, and empty calorie concerns as sugar. Dogs with diabetes mellitus should avoid high-glycemic ingredients. For cats, corn syrup serves zero palatability purpose (Tas1R2 pseudogene) and represents rapidly absorbed glucose for an obligate carnivore with limited glucokinase activity and persistent gluconeogenesis. Particularly concerning for feline metabolic health given the rising incidence of feline Type 2 diabetes. Any humectant function could be achieved through other means.
citations_display: Li X et al. PLoS Genetics. 2005;1(1):e3; Öhlund M et al. J Vet Intern Med. 2015;29(5):1342–1347; AAFCO Official Feed Terms and Ingredient Definitions
position_context: This ingredient's impact scales with amount. Corn syrup listed high on the label contributes meaningful empty calories with high glycemic impact; trace amounts are less concerning — though its presence at any level signals formulation priorities.

definition: Fruit providing pectin (soluble fermentable prebiotic fiber), vitamin C, and polyphenols including quercetin and chlorogenic acid. Seeds removed in commercial processing.
tldr: A functional fiber source with polyphenol content. Dogs metabolize apple polyphenols via functional hepatic glucuronidation. Cats derive fiber benefit from pectin but limited polyphenol benefit because cats lack the liver enzyme needed to fully process these plant compounds.
detail_body: Apples provide pectin (a soluble fermentable prebiotic fiber supporting colonic SCFA production), vitamin C, and polyphenols including quercetin and chlorogenic acid. Seeds contain amygdalin but are removed in commercial processing. Dogs metabolize quercetin and other apple polyphenols via functional hepatic glucuronidation and sulfation pathways; pectin fiber supports fecal quality and GI health. Cats derive fiber benefit from pectin, but polyphenol metabolism is limited by UGT1A6 pseudogene status, reducing systemic bioavailability of phenolic metabolites. Beta-carotene present provides no vitamin A value to cats. Safe at typical inclusion levels but offers limited species-specific benefit beyond fiber for cats.
citations_display: NRC, Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats, 2006; Court MH & Greenblatt DJ, Pharmacogenetics 2000;10:355-369
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Higher on the label = more benefit.

definition: Fruit providing potassium (~358 mg/100g), vitamin B6, and soluble fiber (pectin, resistant starch). Functions primarily as a carbohydrate and fiber source.
tldr: A safe carbohydrate and fiber source for both dogs and cats. Provides potassium and B6 but is not a critical nutrient source at typical pet food inclusion levels.
detail_body: Bananas provide potassium (~358 mg/100g), vitamin B6, and soluble fiber (pectin, resistant starch). They function primarily as a carbohydrate and fiber source with moderate glycemic index in pet food. Dogs tolerate bananas well; potassium and B6 content contribute modestly to daily requirements. Excessive intake may cause soft stools due to soluble fiber content, but standard pet food inclusion levels are not clinically concerning. For cats, potassium and B6 contribute to but are not essential sources of these nutrients in balanced feline diets. No known toxicity concerns at pet food inclusion levels.
citations_display: NRC, Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats, 2006; Hand MS et al., Small Animal Clinical Nutrition, 5th ed., 2010
position_context: A functional ingredient typically included at minor levels. Position on the label has limited scoring impact.

definition: Fruit that is ~92% water by weight, containing lycopene (non-provitamin A carotenoid antioxidant), L-citrulline, and beta-carotene. Seeds and rind excluded in pet food processing.
tldr: Mostly water with some lycopene antioxidant content. Dogs can convert the beta-carotene to vitamin A; cats cannot. Safe but nutritionally unremarkable at typical inclusion levels for both species.
detail_body: Watermelon is ~92% water by weight, containing lycopene (a non-provitamin A carotenoid antioxidant), L-citrulline, and beta-carotene. Seeds and rind are excluded in pet food processing. Dogs convert beta-carotene to retinol at ~5-15% efficiency; lycopene functions as an antioxidant without requiring metabolic conversion. No known toxicity at typical inclusion levels. Cats cannot convert beta-carotene to retinol due to negligible BCO1 activity; beta-carotene in watermelon provides no vitamin A value. Lycopene retains antioxidant function independent of vitamin A conversion. Safe at pet food inclusion levels.
citations_display: Schweigert FJ et al., J Nutr 2002;132(6 Suppl 2):1610S-1612S; NRC 2006
position_context: A functional ingredient typically included at minor levels. Position on the label has limited scoring impact.

definition: Coconut flesh containing medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs) with lauric acid (~48% of total fatty acids), dietary fiber (~9 g/100g), and manganese. MCTs are absorbed via the portal vein.
tldr: A source of medium-chain triglycerides and fiber. MCTs are efficiently absorbed by both species, but coconut provides no essential fatty acids (no EPA/DHA, no arachidonic acid). Does not fulfill the physiological requirement for animal-derived fats.
detail_body: Coconut flesh contains medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs) with lauric acid (~48% of total fatty acids), dietary fiber (~9 g/100g), and manganese. MCTs are absorbed via the portal vein and metabolized hepatically without bile salt emulsification. Dogs efficiently absorb and metabolize MCTs; lauric acid demonstrates in vitro antimicrobial properties. High fat content (~33% in dried coconut) contributes to caloric density; excessive intake may cause GI upset. Cats metabolize MCTs via hepatic beta-oxidation. Coconut flesh provides no essential fatty acids (no EPA/DHA, no arachidonic acid). Well-tolerated at standard inclusion levels; does not fulfill the physiological requirement for animal-derived fats.
citations_display: NRC, Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats, 2006; Hand MS et al., Small Animal Clinical Nutrition, 5th ed., 2010
position_context: A functional ingredient typically included at minor levels. Position on the label has limited scoring impact.

definition: Fruit containing punicalagins (high-molecular-weight polyphenols), ellagic acid, and anthocyanins. Punicalagins are hydrolyzed to ellagic acid and metabolized by colonic microflora to urolithins.
tldr: Rich in punicalagins that are metabolized to bioactive urolithins via hepatic glucuronidation. Dogs have functional UGT1A isoforms for this pathway. Cats lack the liver enzyme needed to fully process these polyphenols, limiting their benefit.
detail_body: Pomegranate contains punicalagins (high-molecular-weight polyphenols, MW >1000), ellagic acid, and anthocyanins. Punicalagins are hydrolyzed to ellagic acid in the GI tract and subsequently metabolized by colonic microflora to urolithins, which undergo hepatic glucuronidation prior to systemic circulation. Dogs possess functional UGT1A6 and ~10 UGT1A isoforms enabling normal Phase II conjugation of ellagic acid metabolites and urolithins; punicalagin-derived metabolites retain bioactive antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Feline UGT1A6 pseudogene and expression of only 2 functional UGT1A isoforms significantly impairs glucuronidation of phenolic metabolites derived from punicalagins, potentially altering clearance kinetics. No direct feline toxicity reported at pet food inclusion levels, but reduced polyphenol detoxification capacity limits classification to neutral.
citations_display: Court MH & Greenblatt DJ, Pharmacogenetics 2000;10:355-369; Cerda B et al., Eur J Nutr 2003;42:18-28
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Higher on the label = more benefit.

definition: Fruit containing papain (a cysteine protease), vitamin C, and beta-carotene. Papain has limited proteolytic activity after gastric acid exposure and thermal processing.
tldr: Contains papain enzyme and beta-carotene, but papain is largely inactivated during kibble manufacture. Both dogs and cats synthesize endogenous vitamin C. Functions primarily as a fiber and flavor component at typical inclusion levels.
detail_body: Papaya contains papain (a cysteine protease with proteolytic activity), vitamin C, and beta-carotene. Papain has been investigated as a digestive enzyme supplement in veterinary contexts. The fruit also provides soluble fiber and potassium. Papain retains limited proteolytic activity after gastric acid exposure and thermal processing in kibble manufacture. Dogs synthesize endogenous vitamin C and do not require dietary supplementation. At standard inclusion levels, papaya functions primarily as a fiber and flavor component. Cats cannot efficiently convert beta-carotene to retinol, so beta-carotene in papaya provides no vitamin A value. Papain is unlikely to survive commercial thermal processing at functionally significant levels. Safe at typical inclusion levels.
citations_display: NRC, Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats, 2006; Schweigert FJ et al., J Nutr 2002;132(6 Suppl 2):1610S-1612S
position_context: A functional ingredient typically included at minor levels. Position on the label has limited scoring impact.

definition: Fruit providing beta-carotene, vitamin C, and polyphenols (mangiferin, quercetin). Pit excluded from pet food processing.
tldr: Provides beta-carotene and polyphenols. Dogs derive modest vitamin A value via BCO1 conversion; cats cannot. Cats lack the liver enzyme needed to fully process these polyphenols. Safe but nutritionally unremarkable for both species.
detail_body: Mango provides beta-carotene, vitamin C, and polyphenols (mangiferin, quercetin). The pit contains amygdalin and is excluded from pet food processing. Dogs convert beta-carotene to retinol at ~5-15% efficiency, deriving modest vitamin A value; polyphenol glucuronidation proceeds normally. No known toxicity at pet food inclusion levels. For cats, beta-carotene provides no vitamin A value due to negligible BCO1 activity. Polyphenol metabolism is limited by UGT1A6 pseudogene status, reducing clearance of mangiferin and quercetin conjugates. Safe at typical inclusion levels but confers minimal species-specific nutritional benefit.
citations_display: NRC, Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats, 2006; Court MH & Greenblatt DJ, Pharmacogenetics 2000;10:355-369
position_context: A functional ingredient typically included at minor levels. Position on the label has limited scoring impact.

definition: AAFCO-defined dried marine algae (Ascophyllum nodosum, Laminaria spp.) used primarily as a natural iodine source. Iodine content varies widely by species and harvest location.
tldr: A whole-food iodine source supporting thyroid function in both species. Iodine content is highly variable between kelp species. Contraindicated in cats with hyperthyroidism or on iodine-restricted therapeutic diets.
detail_body: Dried kelp is an AAFCO-defined dried marine algae used primarily as a natural iodine source. Iodine content varies by species, harvest location, and season: A. nodosum averages ~646 µg/g (±392), while Laminaria spp. can exceed 1,259-4,500+ µg/g. Kelp also provides trace minerals, fucoidan polysaccharides, and demonstrates anti-plaque dental health benefits. Dogs benefit from kelp as a functional whole-food iodine source supporting thyroid hormone synthesis (NRC 2006 RA: 0.88 mg/kg DM; AAFCO max: 11 mg/kg DM). Iodine content variability necessitates manufacturer standardization. For cats, kelp is an effective iodine source (NRC 2006 RA: 350 µg/1000 kcal for adults). However, feline hyperthyroidism is the most common endocrinopathy in cats >6 years, and fluctuating or excessive dietary iodine has been implicated as a contributing factor. Kelp is contraindicated in cats on iodine-restricted therapeutic diets.
citations_display: NRC, Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats, 2006; Edinboro CH et al., J Feline Med Surg 2010;12:672-679
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Higher on the label = more benefit.

definition: AAFCO-defined dehydrated alfalfa meal: aerial portion of the alfalfa plant. Provides ~13-22% crude protein, 20-30% crude fiber, chlorophyll, vitamins A (as beta-carotene) and K, calcium. is_legume: TRUE.
tldr: A legume-family fiber and mineral source. Contributes trace nutrients and chlorophyll at typical inclusion levels. Contains phytoestrogens whose clearance may be slower in cats due to impaired glucuronidation. Counted as a legume for DCM Rule #9.
detail_body: AAFCO-defined dehydrated alfalfa meal: aerial portion of the alfalfa plant, sun-cured or dehydrated and finely ground. Provides ~13-22% crude protein, 20-30% crude fiber, chlorophyll, vitamins A (as beta-carotene) and K, calcium. Contains saponins (which may modulate fat-soluble nutrient absorption) and phytoestrogens (isoflavonoids). At typical pet food inclusion levels (minor ingredient), dogs derive fiber, trace minerals, and chlorophyll; saponins and phytoestrogens are present at sub-clinical concentrations. High calcium content may be relevant for breeds predisposed to calcium urolithiasis. Cats derive no vitamin A from beta-carotene in alfalfa. Phytoestrogens undergo hepatic glucuronidation for clearance — a pathway compromised in cats — theoretically prolonging systemic exposure. High calcium content is relevant given feline CaOx predisposition. Safe at typical minor inclusion levels in balanced diets.
citations_display: NRC, Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats, 2006; Lulich JP et al., JVIM 2016;30:1564-1574
position_context: A functional ingredient typically included at minor levels. Position on the label has limited scoring impact.

definition: AAFCO-defined dried mixture of tomato skins, pulp, and crushed seeds. Contains ~20-24% crude protein, 26-48% crude fiber, and concentrated lycopene. Derived from ripe tomatoes; tomatine/solanine absent.
tldr: A functional fiber source with concentrated lycopene antioxidant. Lycopene bioavailability increases with heat processing. Dogs benefit from fiber and lycopene; cats derive limited benefit from plant fiber beyond fecal bulking and hairball transit.
detail_body: AAFCO-defined dried mixture of tomato skins, pulp, and crushed seeds from commercial processing. Contains approximately 20-24% crude protein, 26-48% crude fiber, and concentrated lycopene (a non-provitamin A carotenoid antioxidant; 36.7-50.2 g/kg). Derived from ripe tomatoes at processing maturity; tomatine/solanine is absent — these glycoalkaloids are present only in immature green fruit and foliage. Tomato pomace serves as a functional source of soluble and insoluble fiber supporting fecal quality and colonic health in dogs. Lycopene is bioavailable and retains antioxidant activity after thermal processing (bioavailability increases with heat). For cats, lycopene does not require conversion (unlike beta-carotene) and functions as an antioxidant, but feline obligate carnivore physiology derives limited benefit from plant fiber beyond fecal bulking and hairball transit facilitation.
citations_display: AAFCO Official Publication, Chapter 6 (Ingredient Definitions); ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Higher on the label = more benefit.

definition: Pumpkin flesh providing a blend of soluble and insoluble fiber, beta-carotene, potassium, and iron. Dual-fiber profile enables bidirectional GI modulation.
tldr: One of the most versatile functional fibers in pet nutrition. Soluble fiber firms loose stools; insoluble fiber relieves constipation. Used in veterinary practice for both conditions in dogs and cats. Low caloric density supports weight management.
detail_body: Pumpkin flesh provides a blend of soluble and insoluble fiber, beta-carotene, potassium, and iron. Its dual-fiber profile enables bidirectional GI modulation: soluble fiber absorbs excess luminal water and ferments to produce SCFAs, while insoluble fiber adds fecal bulk and promotes colonic motility. Pumpkin is utilized in veterinary practice for management of both acute mild diarrhea (soluble fiber absorbs water, firms stool) and constipation (insoluble fiber adds bulk, promotes motility) in dogs. Low caloric density supports use as a caloric diluent in canine weight management protocols. Pumpkin provides comparable GI fiber benefits in cats including fecal bulking and water absorption. Commonly utilized in veterinary practice for feline constipation management. Beta-carotene content has negligible vitamin A value for cats; preformed vitamin A must be provided separately.
citations_display: Hand MS et al., Small Animal Clinical Nutrition, 5th ed., 2010; Fritsch DA et al., BMC Vet Res 2022;18:245
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Higher on the label = more benefit.

definition: Leafy green containing high concentrations of oxalic acid (~970 mg/100g raw weight), along with iron, folate, lutein, and vitamin K.
tldr: One of the highest dietary oxalate sources. Oxalate binds calcium and contributes to urinary stone formation. A theoretical concern for stone-prone dog breeds; a genuine clinical concern for cats given that calcium oxalate accounts for ~40-50% of feline uroliths.
detail_body: Spinach contains high concentrations of oxalic acid (~970 mg/100g raw weight), along with iron, folate, lutein, and vitamin K. Oxalate binds dietary calcium in the GI tract to form insoluble calcium oxalate complexes, reducing calcium bioavailability and contributing exogenous oxalate substrate for urinary stone formation. At typical pet food inclusion levels in dogs, oxalate load represents a theoretical concern in breeds predisposed to CaOx urolithiasis (Miniature Schnauzers, Bichon Frises, Shih Tzus). The University of Minnesota Urolith Center lists spinach as a high-oxalate food to avoid in stone-prone patients. For cats, calcium oxalate is one of the two most prevalent feline urolith types, accounting for approximately 40-50% of feline urocystoliths. Spinach is among the highest dietary oxalate sources and is explicitly identified as a food to restrict in feline CaOx risk-management protocols. Cats are additionally predisposed to idiopathic hypercalcemia (~30% of CaOx cases), compounding risk.
citations_display: Lulich JP et al., JVIM 2016;30:1564-1574; Bartges JW, J Feline Med Surg 2016;18:712-722
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Listed lower on the label = less concern.

definition: Root vegetable providing beta-carotene (~8,285 µg/100g), soluble fiber (pectin), potassium, and vitamin K. In species with functional BCO1, beta-carotene is cleaved to retinal.
tldr: A provitamin A and fiber source for dogs. Dogs possess functional BCO1 enzyme to convert beta-carotene to retinol. Cats lack this enzyme and derive no vitamin A value from carrots. Safe fiber source for both species.
detail_body: Carrots provide beta-carotene (~8,285 µg/100g), soluble fiber (pectin), potassium, and vitamin K. In species with functional BCO1, beta-carotene is symmetrically cleaved in intestinal epithelium to yield two molecules of retinal (vitamin A precursor). Dogs possess functional beta-carotene 15,15'-dioxygenase, enabling conversion of dietary beta-carotene to retinol. Carrots provide provitamin A activity and soluble fiber. Low caloric density (~41 kcal/100g) supports use in weight management. Cats have very low BCO1 activity and cannot efficiently convert beta-carotene to retinol. A stable-isotope study confirmed trace but nutritionally inadequate conversion of beta-carotene to retinol in cats. Carrots provide fiber and minor micronutrients but no functional provitamin A value; preformed retinol remains an obligate dietary requirement.
citations_display: NRC, Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats, 2006; Green AS et al., Br J Nutr 2012;107:1658-1662
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Higher on the label = more benefit.

definition: Cruciferous vegetable containing glucosinolates (primarily glucoraphanin) hydrolyzed by myrosinase to isothiocyanates, notably sulforaphane.
tldr: Contains sulforaphane, an antioxidant pathway activator. Safe when comprising less than 10% of daily caloric intake; amounts exceeding 25% are potentially toxic. Isothiocyanates are conjugated via glutathione pathways (not glucuronidation), so the feline liver enzyme limitation relevant to other plant compounds is not directly applicable here.
detail_body: Broccoli contains glucosinolates (primarily glucoraphanin) hydrolyzed by myrosinase to isothiocyanates, notably sulforaphane — a potent Nrf2 antioxidant-response pathway activator with anti-inflammatory and anticarcinogenic properties in experimental models. Isothiocyanates cause dose-dependent gastric irritation. Per ASPCA guidance, broccoli is safe when comprising less than 10% of daily caloric intake; amounts exceeding 25% of diet are potentially toxic. At typical pet food inclusion levels (well below 10%), broccoli provides sulforaphane, vitamin C, and fiber without clinically significant GI effects. Glucosinolates may impair thyroid iodine uptake at high chronic doses. For cats, no species-specific contraindications at standard inclusion levels. Isothiocyanates are conjugated primarily via glutathione-S-transferase pathways rather than glucuronidation, so feline UGT1A6 deficiency is not directly relevant.
citations_display: ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center; Raghavan M et al., JAVMA 2005;227:94-100
position_context: A functional ingredient typically included at minor levels. Position on the label has limited scoring impact.

definition: Non-spore-forming lactic acid bacterium (AAFCO-listed) with documented immunomodulatory properties but significant viability concerns in extruded dry pet food due to thermal destruction during processing.
tldr: A well-studied probiotic with proven immune and digestive benefits — when it actually survives processing. The catch: L. acidophilus doesn't form protective spores, so it's routinely killed during kibble extrusion. Weese & Arroyo (2003) found none of 19 tested pet foods contained all listed probiotic organisms. A label listing does not guarantee live bacteria at the point of consumption.
detail_body: Lactobacillus acidophilus is a non-spore-forming, gram-positive, facultatively anaerobic lactic acid bacterium and AAFCO-listed direct-fed microorganism. In dogs, L. acidophilus DSM13241 increased fecal lactobacilli, decreased clostridia, and improved immune parameters (Baillon et al., 2004). In cats, Marshall-Jones et al. (2006) demonstrated altered GI microbiota (increased lactobacilli, decreased Clostridium spp.), decreased fecal pH and plasma endotoxin, and immunomodulatory changes at 2 × 10⁸ CFU/day. However, Weese & Arroyo (2003) found none of 19 commercial pet foods claiming probiotics contained all listed organisms; viability of non-spore-forming probiotics in extruded kibble is highly unreliable. A label listing this ingredient does not guarantee live organisms at the point of consumption. Beneficial rating reflects the ingredient's clinical value when viable, with the viability caveat noted.
citations_display: Weese JS, Arroyo L. Bacteriological evaluation of dog and cat diets that claim to contain probiotics. Can Vet J. 2003;44(3):212-216. · Weese JS. Microbiologic evaluation of commercial probiotics. J Am Vet Med Assoc. 2002;220(6):794-797. · Marshall-Jones ZV et al. Effects of Lactobacillus acidophilus DSM13241 as a probiotic in healthy adult cats. Am J Vet Res. 2006;67(6):1005-1012. · Baillon ML et al. Effects of probiotic Lactobacillus acidophilus strain DSM13241 in healthy adult dogs. Am J Vet Res. 2004;65(3):338-343.
position_context: This ingredient is beneficial regardless of position. Probiotics are added as precise supplements — though viability in extruded kibble is unreliable for non-spore-forming organisms.

definition: Cruciferous vegetable containing S-methylcysteine sulfoxide (SMCO), glucosinolates, calcium oxalate, and vitamins K, A, and C. SMCO metabolizes to dimethyl disulfide, a hemoglobin oxidant.
tldr: Contains SMCO, which is metabolized to an oxidant that can denature hemoglobin and produce Heinz bodies. Dogs have 4 reactive sulfhydryl groups on hemoglobin and tolerate typical inclusion levels. Cats have 8 reactive sulfhydryl groups, making them significantly more susceptible to oxidative hemolysis.
detail_body: Kale contains S-methylcysteine sulfoxide (SMCO), glucosinolates, calcium oxalate, and vitamins K, A, and C. SMCO is metabolized by gut microbiota to dimethyl disulfide, an oxidant that denatures hemoglobin by oxidizing sulfhydryl groups on the globin moiety, producing Heinz bodies. At typical pet food inclusion levels in dogs, SMCO and isothiocyanate content is insufficient to induce clinically significant oxidative hemolysis. Canine hemoglobin has 4 reactive sulfhydryl groups per tetramer, conferring lower susceptibility to oxidative denaturation. Standard processing may partially reduce glucosinolate content. Feline hemoglobin has 8 reactive sulfhydryl groups per tetramer, rendering it significantly more susceptible to oxidative denaturation and Heinz body formation. The feline spleen is non-sinusoidal and inefficient at removing Heinz body-containing erythrocytes. Feline UGT1A6 pseudogene status potentially prolongs exposure to oxidant metabolites. Kale is listed among triggers for feline Heinz body anemia alongside onions, garlic, and propylene glycol.
citations_display: Christopher MM et al., Vet Pathol 1990;27:299-310; Christopher MM, JAVMA 1989;194:1089-1095
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Listed lower on the label = less concern.

definition: Immature-harvested legumes providing dietary fiber (~2.7 g/100g), protein (~1.8 g/100g), vitamins A, C, and K at low caloric density (~31 kcal/100g). is_legume: TRUE.
tldr: A recognized veterinary weight management tool for dogs via caloric substitution (the "green bean diet"). Provides low-calorie fiber. Cats derive limited benefit as obligate carnivores. Counted as a legume for DCM Rule #9.
detail_body: Green beans are immature-harvested legumes providing dietary fiber (~2.7 g/100g), protein (~1.8 g/100g), vitamins A, C, and K at low caloric density (~31 kcal/100g). They function primarily as a caloric diluent and fiber source. For dogs, partial caloric substitution with green beans (10-50% volume replacement) is a recognized veterinary weight management strategy to reduce intake while maintaining satiety via fiber-induced gastric distension. Aggressive substitution (>20% of diet) without reformulation risks essential nutrient deficiencies; use requires veterinary supervision. For cats, non-toxic and can serve as low-calorie fiber supplement, but as obligate carnivores, cats have higher protein requirements and limited capacity to derive nutritional benefit from plant-based volume replacement. Green beans lack adequate taurine, arachidonic acid, and preformed vitamin A to support feline-specific needs.
citations_display: Hand MS et al., Small Animal Clinical Nutrition, 5th ed., 2010; ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Higher on the label = more benefit.

definition: Approximately 95% water with a caloric density of ~17 kcal/100g. Provides modest potassium (~261 mg/100g), manganese, vitamin C, and folate.
tldr: Extremely low caloric density and high water content. Suitable as a low-calorie treat addition but nutritionally insignificant at typical pet food inclusion levels for both dogs and cats.
detail_body: Zucchini is approximately 95% water with a caloric density of ~17 kcal/100g. It provides modest amounts of potassium (~261 mg/100g), manganese, vitamin C, and folate. Minimal bioactive compound content; functions primarily as a low-calorie hydration source. Non-toxic, easily digestible, and well-tolerated in dogs. Very low caloric density and high water content make it suitable as a low-calorie treat or volumetric addition to weight management diets. Nutritional contribution at typical inclusion levels is minimal. For cats, non-toxic and provides hydration contribution through high moisture content. Does not address feline-specific nutrient requirements (taurine, arachidonic acid, preformed retinol). No species-specific contraindications.

definition: AAFCO-defined dried residue from sugar beet after sucrose extraction. Contains ~16-24% soluble fiber (pectins) and ~55-75% insoluble fiber (cellulose, hemicellulose), yielding a ~20:80 soluble:insoluble ratio.
tldr: One of the most extensively studied fiber sources in companion animal nutrition. Produces beneficial short-chain fatty acid profiles supporting colonic health in both dogs and cats. Supports fecal consistency without excessive stool softening or nutrient dilution.
detail_body: AAFCO-defined dried residue from sugar beet (Beta vulgaris) after sucrose extraction. One of the most extensively studied fiber sources in companion animal nutrition. Contains approximately 16-24% soluble fiber (pectins) and 55-75% insoluble fiber (cellulose, hemicellulose), yielding a soluble:insoluble ratio of ~20:80. Residual sugar ~5%. Colonic microbial fermentation of the soluble fraction produces SCFAs, predominantly acetate and butyrate, serving as primary energy substrate for colonocytes. In vitro fermentability using dog fecal inocula is 33-38% organic matter disappearance. Graded dietary inclusion (0-12.5%) produces beneficial SCFA profiles supporting colonic health. One consideration: beet pulp may decrease taurine status in dogs fed low-protein diets via enhanced microbial destruction of taurine conjugates. In vitro organic matter disappearance using cat fecal inocula is 35-42%, confirming moderate fermentability. Demonstrated increased colonic SCFA absorption and sustained reduction in fecal pathogenic microbial populations in cats.
citations_display: Sunvold GD et al., J Anim Sci 1995;73:1099-1109; Fahey GC et al., J Anim Sci 1990;68:4221-4228
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Higher on the label = more benefit.

definition: Approximately 95% water with a caloric density of ~16 kcal/100g. Contains vitamin K (~29.3 µg/100g), potassium, folate, and phthalides.
tldr: Extremely low nutrient density; nutritionally insignificant at typical pet food inclusion levels. Some urolith centers list celery among moderate-oxalate foods. No species-specific contraindications.
detail_body: Celery is approximately 95% water with a caloric density of ~16 kcal/100g. Contains vitamin K (~29.3 µg/100g), potassium, folate, and phthalides (3-n-butylphthalide). Nutritional contribution in pet food at typical inclusion levels is minimal. Non-toxic and well-tolerated in dogs. Fibrous texture may present a choking hazard if pieces are not appropriately sized for small breeds. Some urolith centers list celery among moderate-oxalate foods to limit in CaOx-prone dogs. Nutritional significance is negligible. For cats, non-toxic; extremely low nutrient density renders it nutritionally insignificant in feline diets. Phthalide content is insufficient to exert pharmacological effect at dietary levels. No feline-specific contraindications.
citations_display: ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center; NRC, Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats, 2006
position_context: A functional ingredient typically included at minor levels. Position on the label has limited scoring impact.

definition: Culinary parsley (Petroselinum crispum) containing vitamins K and C, apigenin, chlorophyll, and furanocoumarins. Not to be confused with toxic spring parsley (Cymopterus watsonii).
tldr: Provides trace micronutrients and chlorophyll at typical inclusion levels. Furanocoumarin content is insufficient to cause photosensitization at dietary concentrations. Must be distinguished from toxic spring parsley (Cymopterus watsonii), a different species.
detail_body: Culinary parsley (Petroselinum crispum) contains vitamins K (~1,640 µg/100g) and C (~133 mg/100g), the flavonoid apigenin, chlorophyll, and furanocoumarins (photosensitizing agents). Critical distinction: Petroselinum crispum (culinary parsley, the pet food ingredient) must not be confused with spring parsley (Cymopterus watsonii), a toxic plant of the western US causing photosensitization dermatitis — classified toxic to dogs, cats, and horses by ASPCA. At typical pet food inclusion levels, culinary parsley provides trace micronutrients and chlorophyll (utilized for breath-freshening properties in some formulations). Furanocoumarin content at standard dietary concentrations is insufficient to cause photosensitization. For cats, no species-specific toxicity concerns at standard inclusion levels. Feline UGT1A6 deficiency may theoretically slow glucuronidation of apigenin, but at typical dietary concentrations this is not clinically significant.
citations_display: ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center; NRC, Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats, 2006
position_context: A functional ingredient typically included at minor levels. Position on the label has limited scoring impact.

definition: Ground flaxseed containing ~18-23% ALA (alpha-linolenic acid, omega-3), mucilage soluble fiber, ~20% protein, and lignans. Cyanogenic glycosides deactivated by heat processing.
tldr: The richest seed source of plant-based omega-3 (ALA). Dogs convert ALA to EPA at ~5-15% efficiency, providing measurable omega-3 benefit. Cats cannot convert ALA to EPA/DHA because they lack the enzyme needed to make this conversion, so flaxseed provides no functional long-chain omega-3 benefit to cats.
detail_body: Ground flaxseed contains ~18-23% ALA (alpha-linolenic acid, omega-3), mucilage soluble fiber, ~20% protein, and lignans (secoisolariciresinol diglucoside) with phytoestrogenic and antioxidant properties. Raw seed contains cyanogenic glycosides (linamarin, lotaustralin); these are deactivated by heat processing and grinding. Dogs convert dietary ALA to EPA at ~5-15% efficiency via delta-6 desaturase, providing modest but measurable functional omega-3 benefit. Canine feeding studies demonstrate early and sustained serum EPA and DPA enrichment following flaxseed supplementation. Mucilage fiber and lignans contribute additional soluble fiber and antioxidant capacity. Flaxseed should not serve as sole EPA/DHA source. Cats have virtually no delta-6 desaturase activity and cannot convert ALA to EPA or DHA; flaxseed provides no functional long-chain omega-3 fatty acids to cats. Flaxseed does increase serum ALA and provides soluble fiber benefit, but cats require preformed EPA/DHA from marine sources.
citations_display: Bauer JE, JAVMA 2006;229:1729-1732; Dunbar BL et al., Lipids 2010;45:1-10
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Higher on the label = more benefit.

definition: Seeds containing ~18-20% ALA, extensive hydrophilic mucilage gel formation (absorbs up to 12x weight in water), high calcium and phosphorus, and antioxidant polyphenols. Gluten-free.
tldr: Similar ALA profile to flaxseed with added mineral density and mucilage fiber. Dogs convert ALA to EPA at ~5-15% efficiency. Cats cannot convert ALA to functional omega-3s. High phosphorus content warrants consideration in cats with compromised renal function.
detail_body: Chia seeds contain ~18-20% ALA, extensive hydrophilic mucilage gel formation (absorbs up to 12x weight in water), high calcium (~456-631 mg/100g), phosphorus (~860-919 mg/100g), and antioxidant polyphenols. Gluten-free with no known toxic compounds at dietary inclusion levels. As with flaxseed, dogs convert ALA to EPA at ~5-15% efficiency. Chia provides highly bioavailable soluble fiber supporting glycemic modulation and fecal quality, along with substantial mineral density. Seeds should be soaked or milled for optimal digestibility. Cats cannot convert ALA to EPA/DHA due to absent delta-6 desaturase activity, so chia provides no functional long-chain omega-3 benefit. May contribute soluble fiber and mineral content, but high phosphorus content warrants consideration in cats with compromised renal function.
citations_display: Bauer JE, JAVMA 2006;229:1729-1732; NRC, Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats, 2006
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Higher on the label = more benefit.

definition: Pseudocereal (seed, not a true grain) containing all essential amino acids (~14% crude protein), dietary fiber, B vitamins, iron, magnesium. Gluten-free. Saponin coating removed by processing.
tldr: A gluten-free pseudocereal with a complete amino acid profile, making it nutritionally superior to most cereal grains as a protein complement. Safe and well-tolerated at typical inclusion levels for both species.
detail_body: Pseudocereal (seed, not a true grain) containing all essential amino acids (complete protein profile, ~14% crude protein), dietary fiber, B vitamins, iron, magnesium, and antioxidant flavonoids. Saponin coating is removed by washing and/or destroyed during extrusion processing. Gluten-free. Well-tolerated, highly digestible carbohydrate source when properly processed in dogs. Complete amino acid profile makes it nutritionally superior to most cereal grains as a protein complement. Saponin content in unprocessed quinoa may cause GI irritation; commercial pet food processing adequately mitigates this risk. For cats, provides digestible, gluten-free carbohydrate with complete plant protein. As obligate carnivores, cats derive primary amino acid requirements from animal-source proteins; quinoa's contribution is supplementary. No toxicity concerns at typical inclusion levels.
citations_display: NRC, Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats, 2006; Hand MS et al., Small Animal Clinical Nutrition, 5th ed., 2010
position_context: A functional ingredient typically included at minor levels. Position on the label has limited scoring impact.

definition: Quaternary ammonium compound essential for mitochondrial long-chain fatty acid transport. Approximately 60% of myocardial energy derives from fatty acid β-oxidation. Conditionally essential during obesity, hepatic lipidosis, caloric restriction, and cardiac disease.
tldr: Essential for transporting fat into mitochondria for energy production — approximately 60% of heart energy comes from this pathway. Indicated in canine weight management and cardiomyopathy (especially Boxers). Critically important for cats: L-carnitine supplementation significantly reduced ketosis during fasting-induced hepatic lipidosis, the most common fatal liver disease in cats.
detail_body: L-Carnitine (β-hydroxy-γ-trimethylaminobutyric acid) is essential for mitochondrial long-chain fatty acid transport via the carnitine-palmitoyl transferase (CPT) shuttle system. Approximately 60% of myocardial energy derives from fatty acid β-oxidation, making carnitine conditionally essential during obesity, hepatic lipidosis, caloric restriction, and cardiac disease. In dogs, it is indicated in weight management (facilitates fat oxidation while preserving lean mass) and cardiomyopathy. Keene et al. (1991) documented myocardial L-carnitine deficiency in Boxers with dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM); supplementation (50–200 mg/kg PO TID) improved myocardial function. Indicated for Boxers, American Cocker Spaniels, and dogs with DCM of uncertain etiology. In cats, it is critically relevant for feline hepatic lipidosis (FHL) risk reduction and obesity management. Blanchard et al. (2002) demonstrated L-carnitine supplementation (1000 mg/kg diet) significantly reduced ketosis during fasting-induced FHL. Center (2005) documents 250 mg/day PO as dietary adjunct to promote fatty acid oxidation during FHL recovery.
citations_display: Keene BW et al. Myocardial L-carnitine deficiency in a family of dogs with dilated cardiomyopathy. J Am Vet Med Assoc. 1991;198(4):647-650. · Center SA. Feline hepatic lipidosis. Vet Clin North Am Small Anim Pract. 2005;35(1):225-269. · Blanchard G et al. Effects of dietary L-carnitine supplementation on lipid metabolism in fasting-induced hepatic lipidosis in cats. J Anim Physiol Anim Nutr. 2002;86(3-4):132-140.
position_context: This ingredient is beneficial regardless of position. L-Carnitine is added as a precise nutritional supplement.

definition: Cereal grain containing 3-5% beta-glucan (soluble fiber), a prebiotic substrate producing SCFAs upon colonic fermentation. Contains avenin; classified as gluten-free for most dietary purposes.
tldr: A beta-glucan-rich grain with demonstrated cholesterol reduction and immune modulation benefits in dogs. Oat beta-glucan supplementation significantly reduced serum LDL-c and supported a healthy immune response. Cats derive energy and fiber but limited species-specific benefit.
detail_body: Cereal grain containing 3-5% beta-glucan (1→3, 1→4-linked soluble fiber), a prebiotic substrate producing SCFAs upon colonic fermentation. Contains avenin (a prolamin structurally distinct from wheat gliadin) — classified as gluten-free for most dietary purposes. Provides B vitamins, manganese, selenium, and linoleic acid. Oat beta-glucan supplementation (1% of diet) in dogs significantly reduced serum total cholesterol, LDL-c, and VLDL-c, and positively modulated vaccine-associated immune response (reduced IL-4, shifting Th2→Th1 balance). Oats are highly digestible when cooked and provide a hypoallergenic carbohydrate source. For cats, oats serve as a digestible, gluten-free carbohydrate source. Beta-glucan and soluble fiber may support GI health. No toxicity or adverse effects documented at typical dietary levels. As obligate carnivores, cats have limited carbohydrate requirements; oats function primarily as an energy source and fiber contributor.
citations_display: Ferreira LG et al., PLoS ONE 2018;13(7):e0201133; NRC, Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats, 2006
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Higher on the label = more benefit.

definition: Small-seeded, gluten-free cereal grain providing ~70% starch, ~11% protein, B vitamins, magnesium, phosphorus, and iron. Higher fat content (~4%) than most cereal grains.
tldr: A gluten-free carbohydrate and energy source suitable for dogs with grain sensitivities. Nutritional profile comparable to other cereal grains. Functions as supplementary energy provision for cats.
detail_body: Small-seeded, gluten-free cereal grain providing ~70% starch, ~11% protein, B vitamins (niacin, B6, folate), magnesium, phosphorus, and iron. Higher fat content (~4%) than most cereal grains. No known toxic compounds at dietary inclusion levels. Provides a gluten-free, hypoallergenic carbohydrate and energy source suitable for dogs with grain sensitivities. Nutritional profile comparable to other cereal grains with good relative protein quality. Serves as a functional carbohydrate in extruded formulations without documented adverse effects. For cats, functions as a digestible, gluten-free carbohydrate source. Nutrient contribution is primarily energetic. As obligate carnivores, cats derive essential amino acids and fatty acids from animal-source ingredients; millet's role is supplementary energy provision.
citations_display: NRC, Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats, 2006; Hand MS et al., Small Animal Clinical Nutrition, 5th ed., 2010
position_context: A functional ingredient typically included at minor levels. Position on the label has limited scoring impact.

definition: AAFCO-defined cereal grain containing beta-glucan soluble fiber (~3.8%), ~65-68% starch, ~10-12% protein. Contains hordeins (prolamin storage proteins classified as gluten).
tldr: A digestible carbohydrate with notable beta-glucan content. Hordein (gluten) is a consideration for rare dogs with confirmed gluten-sensitive enteropathy (primarily Irish Setters). Safe for the general population of both dogs and cats.
detail_body: AAFCO-defined cereal grain containing beta-glucan soluble fiber (~3.8%, highest among common cereal grains), ~65-68% starch, ~10-12% protein, B vitamins, selenium, and phosphorus. Contains hordeins — prolamin storage proteins classified as gluten. Well-established, highly digestible carbohydrate source for dogs. Beta-glucan content supports cholesterol metabolism and prebiotic fermentation. Hordein (gluten) content is a consideration for rare dogs with confirmed gluten-sensitive enteropathy (documented primarily in Irish Setters); for the general canine population, barley is well-tolerated. For cats, digestible carbohydrate and energy source. Beta-glucan content may contribute modest prebiotic benefit. Gluten sensitivity has not been documented as a clinically significant condition in cats. Functions as supplementary energy source at typical inclusion levels.
citations_display: NRC, Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats, 2006; Case LP et al., Canine and Feline Nutrition, 3rd ed., Elsevier, 2011
position_context: A functional ingredient typically included at minor levels. Position on the label has limited scoring impact.

definition: Gluten-free cereal grain (~70% starch, ~10-11% protein). Some varieties contain condensed tannins that can reduce digestibility. Low-tannin cultivars preferred for pet food.
tldr: A gluten-free carbohydrate source. Condensed tannins in some varieties decrease digestibility compared to rice, but low-tannin varieties and extrusion processing mitigate anti-nutritional effects. Safe for both species at typical inclusion levels.
detail_body: Gluten-free cereal grain used as a carbohydrate energy source (~70% starch, ~10-11% protein). Some varieties contain condensed tannins (proanthocyanidins) that can precipitate proteins, inhibit digestive enzymes, and chelate trace minerals. Low-tannin cultivars are preferred for pet food. Rich in phenolic phytochemicals with antioxidant activity. Provides a gluten-free carbohydrate source for dogs. Condensed tannins in some varieties decrease apparent digestibility of dry matter, crude protein, fat, and metabolizable energy compared to rice. Low-tannin varieties and extrusion processing mitigate anti-nutritional effects; post-extrusion studies show no digestibility differences between sorghum and maize. Sorghum bran fractions may enhance plasma antioxidant capacity. For cats, tolerated as a carbohydrate source. Condensed tannin content in high-tannin varieties may reduce protein digestibility, which is particularly undesirable given feline higher protein requirements; low-tannin cultivars are preferred for feline formulations.
citations_display: Teixeira L et al., PLoS ONE 2019;14(5):e0208869; NRC, Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats, 2006
position_context: A functional ingredient typically included at minor levels. Position on the label has limited scoring impact.

definition: Cosmetic food colorant produced in four classes (I–IV) with zero nutritional benefit. Classes III and IV contain 4-methylimidazole (4-MEI), classified by IARC as Group 2B (possibly carcinogenic).
tldr: A cosmetic colorant with zero nutritional or functional benefit to the animal. The concern is 4-methylimidazole (4-MEI), a contaminant in Class III/IV caramel color classified as possibly carcinogenic (IARC Group 2B). NTP studies demonstrated clear carcinogenicity in mice at high doses. Pet food labels typically don't specify which class of caramel color is used. Cats don't select food based on color — this ingredient exists purely for owner marketing appeal.
detail_body: Caramel color is a cosmetic food colorant produced in four classes (I–IV) with zero nutritional benefit. Classes III (ammonia caramel) and IV (sulfite ammonia caramel) contain 4-methylimidazole (4-MEI), classified by IARC as Group 2B (possibly carcinogenic, 2011). NTP TR-535 (2007) demonstrated clear evidence of carcinogenicity in B6C3F1 mice (alveolar/bronchiolar neoplasms) at 312–1,250 ppm. California Prop 65 threshold: 29 µg 4-MEI/serving. In dogs, it provides no nutritional or functional benefit. The presence of 4-MEI in Class III/IV with demonstrated lung carcinogenicity in rodents represents an unnecessary risk exposure. Pet food labels typically do not specify caramel color class, preventing consumer assessment of 4-MEI content. In cats, cats lack visual food assessment behavior and do not select food based on color — caramel color is exclusively for owner marketing appeal with zero palatability or nutritional benefit to the animal. Given feline UGT1A6-mediated glucuronidation deficiency, cats may have reduced capacity to metabolize xenobiotic contaminants. Addition of a non-functional ingredient carrying a possible carcinogenic contaminant is clinically unjustifiable in feline diets.
citations_display: National Toxicology Program. Toxicology and Carcinogenesis Studies of 4-Methylimidazole in F344/N Rats and B6C3F1 Mice. NTP TR-535. NIH Publication No. 07-4471; 2007. · IARC Working Group. 4-Methylimidazole. IARC Monographs. 2013;101:447-459.
position_context: This is a presence-based concern. Caramel color provides zero nutritional benefit at any level — any risk from 4-MEI contaminant is unjustifiable.

definition: AAFCO-defined dried, non-fermentative (inactive) yeast from brewing. Contains ~45-52% crude protein, B-complex vitamins, selenium, chromium, and cell wall beta-glucans (~11%) and mannan-oligosaccharides (MOS).
tldr: A concentrated source of B vitamins, protein, and immunomodulatory beta-glucans. Especially important for cats, which require dietary thiamine at approximately 2.5× the canine requirement. Supplemental products containing added garlic are contraindicated in cats due to Heinz body hemolytic anemia risk.
detail_body: AAFCO-defined dried, non-fermentative (inactive) yeast recovered as a by-product of brewing. Contains ~45-52% crude protein, B-complex vitamins (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, pyridoxine, biotin), selenium, chromium, and cell wall beta-glucans (1,3/1,6-beta-D-glucan, ~11%) and mannan-oligosaccharides (MOS) with immunomodulatory and prebiotic properties. Concentrated brewers yeast supplementation (7 mg beta-glucan/kg BW/day) reduced markers of gut permeability and haptoglobin concentrations (indicating reduced systemic inflammation) while increasing fecal fermentation markers in adult sled dogs. Enhances palatability and supports innate immune function via dectin-1 receptor activation on macrophages and neutrophils. Cats cannot efficiently store B vitamins and require dietary thiamine at approximately 2.5× the canine requirement; brewers yeast is one of the richest natural thiamine sources. Provides highly digestible protein, palatability enhancement, and beta-glucan immunomodulatory support. Individual yeast sensitivity is possible; supplemental products containing added garlic are contraindicated due to feline Heinz body hemolytic anemia risk.
citations_display: Rummell RM et al., J Anim Sci 2022;100:skac281; AAFCO Official Publication
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Higher on the label = more benefit.

definition: Saturated fat predominantly composed of MCTs: lauric acid (~48%), myristic acid (~18%), caprylic and capric acid. Contains zero omega-3, zero arachidonic acid, zero linoleic acid.
tldr: A source of medium-chain triglycerides rapidly converted to ketone bodies. Contains zero essential fatty acids. MCT supplementation showed some cognitive benefit in aged dogs, but as a sole fat source coconut oil is nutritionally incomplete for both species.
detail_body: Saturated fat composed predominantly of MCTs: lauric acid (C12:0) ~48%, myristic acid (C14:0) ~18%, caprylic acid (C8:0) and capric acid (C10:0). Contains zero omega-3, zero arachidonic acid, zero linoleic acid — contributes no essential fatty acid value. MCTs are rapidly absorbed without bile acid dependency and converted hepatically to ketone bodies. MCT supplementation (5.5% of diet) improved cognitive test performance and elevated circulating beta-hydroxybutyrate in aged Beagles over 8 months, supporting a role in canine cognitive dysfunction adjunctive nutritional support. As a sole fat source, coconut oil would be nutritionally incomplete. For cats, digestible and tolerable; MCTs are metabolized via hepatic beta-oxidation. Provides zero arachidonic acid (a feline dietary essential) and zero EPA/DHA, which cats cannot synthesize endogenously. Contributes only caloric value and MCT-derived ketones; does not fulfill the physiological requirement for animal-derived fats.
citations_display: Pan Y et al., Br J Nutr 2010;103:1746-1754; NRC, Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats, 2006
position_context: A functional ingredient typically included at minor levels. Position on the label has limited scoring impact.

definition: Most concentrated plant-based ALA source (~53% alpha-linolenic acid, ~17% linoleic acid). Contains zero preformed EPA, DHA, or arachidonic acid. Conversion efficiency is species-dependent.
tldr: The most concentrated plant omega-3 oil. Dogs convert ALA to EPA at ~5-15% efficiency, providing limited but measurable benefit. Cats cannot meaningfully convert ALA to EPA or DHA, making flaxseed oil ineffective as an omega-3 source for felines.
detail_body: Most concentrated plant-based ALA source (~53% alpha-linolenic acid, ~17% linoleic acid). Contains zero preformed EPA, DHA, or arachidonic acid. ALA requires enzymatic conversion via delta-6 desaturase to yield biologically active long-chain omega-3s; conversion efficiency is species-dependent. Dogs convert ALA to EPA at ~5-15% efficiency, providing limited but measurable omega-3 benefit. Also supplies linoleic acid, which dogs convert to arachidonic acid. Contributes to favorable n-6:n-3 dietary ratio and may support skin/coat health, though inferior to marine-source EPA/DHA for anti-inflammatory therapeutic applications. Cats have virtually no functional delta-6 desaturase activity and cannot meaningfully convert ALA to EPA or DHA. Linoleic acid present also cannot be efficiently converted to arachidonic acid in cats. Not harmful, but not an adequate omega-3 source for feline species; does not fulfill the physiological requirement for preformed marine-source EPA/DHA.
citations_display: Bauer JE, JAVMA 2006;229:1729-1732; NRC, Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats, 2006
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Higher on the label = more benefit.

definition: Standard varieties contain 60-70% linoleic acid (LA, omega-6). Contains zero arachidonic acid, zero EPA, zero DHA, negligible ALA. Primarily an omega-6 linoleic acid source.
tldr: A pure omega-6 fat source. Dogs can convert linoleic acid to arachidonic acid; cats cannot. Provides no omega-3 fatty acids. Excessive dietary LA without counterbalancing omega-3s may promote a pro-inflammatory eicosanoid profile in both species.
detail_body: Standard varieties contain 60-70% linoleic acid (LA, omega-6); high-oleic varieties contain ~80% oleic acid (omega-9). Contains zero arachidonic acid, zero EPA, zero DHA, negligible ALA. Serves primarily as an energy-dense source of the essential omega-6 fatty acid linoleic acid for epidermal barrier function and skin/coat integrity. Dogs enzymatically convert linoleic acid to arachidonic acid via functional delta-6 desaturase; suitable omega-6 source. Provides no omega-3; excessive dietary LA without counterbalancing omega-3s may promote a pro-inflammatory eicosanoid profile. For cats, supplies linoleic acid, which is essential. However, cats cannot efficiently convert LA to arachidonic acid due to limited delta-6 desaturase activity. Supports skin barrier function via LA but does not fulfill the feline requirement for preformed arachidonic acid, EPA, or DHA. Safe but nutritionally incomplete as a sole fat source.
citations_display: NRC, Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats, 2006; Lenox CE, JAVMA 2015;246:1198-1202
position_context: A functional ingredient typically included at minor levels. Position on the label has limited scoring impact.

definition: Marine-derived oil providing preformed EPA and DHA in triglyceride form, typically from cold-water species (salmon, menhaden, anchovy, sardine).
tldr: The gold standard omega-3 source for both dogs and cats. Provides preformed EPA and DHA that bypass conversion limitations. In controlled trials, fish oil supplementation significantly improved weight-bearing in dogs with osteoarthritis (5.6% increase in peak vertical force). Essential for cats, which cannot synthesize EPA/DHA from plant sources.
detail_body: Marine-derived oil providing preformed EPA (eicosapentaenoic acid) and DHA (docosahexaenoic acid) in triglyceride form, typically from cold-water species (salmon, menhaden, anchovy, sardine). EPA and DHA are precursors to anti-inflammatory eicosanoids (series-3 prostaglandins, resolvins, protectins) and incorporate directly into cell membrane phospholipids. Quality concerns include heavy metal contamination (mercury, PCBs), oxidative instability, and ethoxyquin preservative use in some sources. Preformed EPA/DHA bypasses the inefficient ALA→EPA conversion pathway. In two randomized, double-blinded, controlled trials (n=127 and n=38), dietary fish oil supplementation significantly improved weight-bearing (5.6% increase in peak vertical force) and clinical signs of osteoarthritis in dogs. Also supports dermatologic, renal, and cardiovascular function. Source quality (purification, heavy metal testing) should be verified. Note: studies funded by a major veterinary pet food manufacturer. Fish oil is an essential source of preformed EPA and DHA for cats, which cannot synthesize these long-chain omega-3s from plant-based ALA. NRC 2006 establishes dietary requirements for EPA+DHA at all feline life stages. Fish oil provides the primary practical dietary route for meeting feline omega-3 requirements.
citations_display: Roush JK et al., JAVMA 2010;236:59-66 (peak vertical force); Roush JK et al., JAVMA 2010;236:67-73 (multicenter); Bauer JE, JAVMA 2006;229:1729-1732
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Higher on the label = more benefit.

definition: Short-chain fructose polymers (DP 2–10) selectively fermented in the colon by beneficial bacteria. More rapidly fermented than long-chain inulin.
tldr: A prebiotic fiber that feeds beneficial gut bacteria. More rapidly fermented than inulin, which means stronger effects but also more gas at higher doses. At moderate inclusion (0.15–0.3% DM) it increases butyrate production and beneficial bacteria without GI upset. Flatulence threshold is approximately 4% DM in dogs.
detail_body: Fructooligosaccharides (FOS) are short-chain fructose polymers (degree of polymerization 2–10) resistant to mammalian enzymatic hydrolysis. They are selectively fermented in the colon by Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus, producing short-chain fatty acids including butyrate. Commercially derived from chicory root hydrolysis or enzymatic synthesis from sucrose. In dogs, Pinna et al. (2018) confirmed increased fecal SCFA (particularly butyrate) and improved mineral absorption at moderate inclusion (0.15–0.3% DM) without GI disturbance; flatulence threshold is approximately 4% DM. In cats, Hesta et al. (2001) demonstrated significant fecal SCFA effects at 3% oligofructose, with 6% and 9% producing notable fecal changes. Inclusion should be conservative (≤3% DM) in cats to avoid osmotic diarrhea given the shorter feline colon. FOS is not a phenolic compound, so feline UGT1A6 deficiency is not a concern.
citations_display: Pinna C et al. Influence of dietary protein and fructooligosaccharides on fecal fermentative end-products, fecal bacterial populations and apparent total tract digestibility in dogs. BMC Vet Res. 2018;14:106. · Hesta M et al. The effect of oligofructose and inulin on faecal characteristics and nutrient digestibility in healthy cats. J Anim Physiol Anim Nutr. 2001;85(5-6):135-141.
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Higher on the label = more prebiotic benefit, but excess can cause flatulence and loose stool.

definition: Long-chain fructan (DP 10–60) from chicory root or Jerusalem artichoke with slower colonic fermentation than FOS, producing more gradual SCFA generation and less gas.
tldr: A long-chain prebiotic fiber often combined with FOS in "full-spectrum" blends. Ferments more slowly than FOS, producing steady short-chain fatty acid levels along the entire colon. Less likely to cause gas than FOS at equivalent doses.
detail_body: Inulin is a long-chain fructan (degree of polymerization 10–60) primarily extracted from chicory root or Jerusalem artichoke. Its slower colonic fermentation compared to FOS results in more gradual SCFA production and reduced gas generation. Often combined with FOS in "full-spectrum" prebiotic blends to optimize fermentation along the entire colon length. In dogs, Propst et al. (2003) demonstrated increased fecal SCFA and decreased fecal phenols at 0.3–0.9% DM with no adverse effects on stool quality; biological responses to inulin are more variable than to oligofructose at equivalent inclusion. In cats, Hesta et al. (2001) found no significant differences between 3% inulin and 3% oligofructose, though oligofructose appeared more readily fermentable. Barry et al. (2014) confirmed fructan supplementation modifies stool metabolite concentrations and fecal microbiota in senior cats. Typical inclusion rates are ≤3% DM.
citations_display: Propst EL et al. A dose-response experiment evaluating the effects of oligofructose and inulin on nutrient digestibility, stool quality, and fecal protein catabolites in healthy adult dogs. J Anim Sci. 2003;81(12):3057-3066. · Barry KA et al. Fructan supplementation of senior cats affects stool metabolite concentrations and fecal microbiota concentrations, but not nitrogen partitioning in excreta. J Anim Sci. 2014;92(11):4964-4971.
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Higher on the label = more prebiotic benefit.

definition: Concentrated fiber extract from Cucurbita spp. providing a mix of soluble and insoluble fiber at higher density than canned pumpkin (~90% moisture). Also supplies beta-carotene.
tldr: A widely utilized fiber source for mild digestive issues in both dogs and cats. The soluble fraction absorbs excess water (helps with diarrhea), while the insoluble fraction adds bulk (helps with constipation). Also used for feline hairball management. Evidence is mostly clinical experience rather than controlled trials.
detail_body: Pumpkin fiber is a concentrated extract from Cucurbita spp. providing higher fiber density than whole canned pumpkin (which is ~90% moisture). It contains a mix of soluble and insoluble fiber: the soluble fraction absorbs excess luminal water (anti-diarrheal effect), while the insoluble fraction adds bulk (anti-constipation effect). Also supplies beta-carotene. In dogs, it is widely utilized in veterinary practice for acute mild diarrhea and mild constipation and is included as a fiber component in therapeutic GI diets. Evidence is predominantly empirical/clinical experience — no randomized controlled trials specifically evaluating pumpkin fiber isolate in dogs are published. In cats, canned pumpkin (not concentrated extract) is referenced in the Merck Veterinary Manual as a fiber supplementation option for constipation alongside psyllium; dosing of the concentrated extract form differs from whole canned pumpkin and should be adjusted for fiber density. Also utilized for hairball management due to fiber-mediated fecal transit promotion. Adequate hydration is necessary in both species.
citations_display: Hand MS et al., eds. Small Animal Clinical Nutrition. 5th ed. Mark Morris Institute; 2010 (Fiber chapter). · Merck Veterinary Manual. Constipation, obstipation, and megacolon in small animals (pumpkin as fiber supplementation option).
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Higher on the label = more fiber benefit.

definition: Highly soluble, highly fermentable polysaccharide from citrus peel or apple pomace that functions as a gelling agent and fiber with documented heavy metal–binding capacity.
tldr: A rapidly fermented soluble fiber used as a gelling agent. Produces abundant short-chain fatty acids but also significant gas, so it works best when blended with moderately fermentable fibers rather than used alone. Has an interesting side benefit of binding heavy metals (lead, cadmium).
detail_body: Pectin is a highly soluble, highly fermentable polysaccharide extracted from citrus peel or apple pomace. It produces predominantly acetate and propionate upon colonic fermentation, and has documented heavy metal–binding capacity (chelation of lead, cadmium, and other divalent cations). In dogs, its rapid fermentability produces abundant SCFA but also rapid gas generation (CO₂, H₂, CH₄), risking flatulence and loose stool at higher inclusion. Best used in blends with moderately fermentable fibers (e.g., beet pulp) rather than as sole fiber source. In cats, it is safe at typical pet food inclusion levels as part of blended fiber formulations. Barry et al. (2010) demonstrated pectin supplementation increases fecal acetate and propionate in cats. Excessive inclusion risks osmotic diarrhea in the shorter feline colon.
citations_display: Barry KA et al. Dietary cellulose, fructo-oligosaccharides, and pectin modify fecal protein catabolites and microbial populations in adult cats. J Anim Sci. 2010;88:2978-2987. · Hand MS et al., eds. Small Animal Clinical Nutrition. 5th ed. Mark Morris Institute; 2010.
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. Higher on the label = more fermentation, gas, and GI effects.

definition: High-molecular-weight galactomannan from guar bean endosperm (family Fabaceae). FDA GRAS. Primarily a thickener, emulsifier, and stabilizer in wet pet foods at 0.01–0.5% inclusion. Technically legume-derived.
tldr: A common thickener in wet pet food. Although technically derived from a legume (guar bean), it's used in trace quantities as a texturizer — not as a primary ingredient — and is not implicated in the FDA's DCM investigation, which focused on pulses as main ingredients.
detail_body: Guar gum is a high-molecular-weight galactomannan (mannose:galactose ratio ~2:1) from guar bean (Cyamopsis tetragonoloba) endosperm, family Fabaceae. FDA GRAS (human food: 21 CFR 184.1339; animal feed: 21 CFR 582.7339). Classified as a highly fermentable, highly viscous soluble fiber. Although botanically legume-derived, it is used in trace quantities (0.01–0.5%) as a texturizer and is not implicated in the FDA DCM investigation, which focused on pulses (peas, lentils, chickpeas) as main ingredients providing substantial dietary legume protein. In dogs, at ≤0.5% it may slightly soften stool but does not jeopardize nutrient supply. EFSA (2022) assessed guar gum safe for all animal species. In cats, effects at typical commercial wet food levels (<0.5%) are minimal in healthy animals. One study found 0.4% DM guar gum had a modest negative effect on apparent protein digestibility, with effects more pronounced in older cats. No phenolic content; no UGT1A6 concerns.
citations_display: EFSA Panel on Additives and Products or Substances used in Animal Feed. Safety and efficacy of guar gum for all animal species. EFSA J. 2022;20(4):e07252. · AAFCO Official Publication (permitted food additive).
position_context: This ingredient is a functional additive used in trace quantities. Its impact is consistent regardless of label position.

definition: High-molecular-weight exopolysaccharide produced by bacterial fermentation. FDA GRAS. Functions as a non-fermentable thickener and stabilizer that passes through the GI tract largely intact.
tldr: A common thickener and stabilizer in wet pet food produced by bacterial fermentation. Unlike most soluble fibers, xanthan gum is non-fermentable — it passes through the GI tract intact without producing gas or affecting stool consistency. Well-tolerated in both dogs and cats at standard inclusion levels.
detail_body: Xanthan gum is a high-molecular-weight exopolysaccharide produced by aerobic bacterial fermentation of Xanthomonas campestris. FDA GRAS (human food: 21 CFR 172.695; animal feed: 21 CFR 573.1010). It functions as a non-fermentable thickener and stabilizer that passes through the GI tract largely intact without significant short-chain fatty acid production. In dogs, it is well-tolerated at typical pet food concentrations with no evidence of adverse effects. In cats, the non-fermentable nature means it does not contribute to osmotic diarrhea. No phenolic content; no UGT1A6, CKD, or urolithiasis concerns.
citations_display: Hand MS et al., eds. Small Animal Clinical Nutrition. 5th ed. Mark Morris Institute; 2010 (xanthan gum classified as low-fermentability fiber). · FDA 21 CFR 172.695 (human food), 21 CFR 573.1010 (animal feed); AAFCO Official Publication.
position_context: This ingredient is a functional additive used in trace quantities. Its impact is consistent regardless of label position.

definition: High-molecular-weight galactomannan from carob seed endosperm (family Fabaceae). FDA GRAS. Synergistic with xanthan gum and carrageenan in wet food texture systems. Technically legume-derived.
tldr: A thickener from carob seeds often paired with xanthan gum in wet pet food. Like guar gum, it's technically legume-derived but used in trace quantities as a texturizer — not a main ingredient. The purified seed gum does not contain the condensed tannins found in carob pod/pulp.
detail_body: Locust bean gum is a high-molecular-weight galactomannan (mannose:galactose ratio ~4:1) from carob seed (Ceratonia siliqua) endosperm, family Fabaceae. FDA GRAS (human food: 21 CFR 184.1343; animal feed: 21 CFR 582.7343). It exhibits synergistic gel-strengthening with xanthan gum and carrageenan, making it a key component in wet pet food texture systems. Although legume-derived, it is a purified seed endosperm extract used in trace quantities (0.01–0.5%) and is not implicated in the FDA DCM investigation. The critical distinction is between the purified seed gum (substantially free of polyphenolic compounds) and carob pod/pulp (which contains condensed tannins). In dogs, GI effects at typical levels are minimal. In cats, EFSA (2017) re-evaluation found locust bean gum non-toxic in acute and chronic studies, and the absence of significant condensed tannins means feline UGT1A6 deficiency is not a relevant concern.
citations_display: EFSA Panel on Food Additives and Nutrient Sources (ANS). Re-evaluation of locust bean gum (E 410) as a food additive. EFSA J. 2017;15(1):e04646. · EFSA FEEDAP Panel. Safety and efficacy of locust bean gum for all animal species. EFSA J. 2022;20(7):e07403.
position_context: This ingredient is a functional additive used in trace quantities. Its impact is consistent regardless of label position.

definition: Spore-forming, lactic acid–producing probiotic (AAFCO-listed) whose endospores demonstrate superior thermostability through kibble extrusion — the most reliable probiotic for extruded dry food.
tldr: The standout probiotic for dry pet food. Unlike most probiotics that are killed during kibble extrusion, B. coagulans forms heat-resistant spores that survive processing and deliver viable organisms to the gut. Weese & Arroyo (2003) found none of 19 commercial pet foods contained all their listed probiotics — B. coagulans is the exception that consistently survives.
detail_body: Bacillus coagulans is a spore-forming, gram-positive, lactic acid–producing probiotic and AAFCO-listed direct-fed microorganism. Its endospores demonstrate superior thermostability through kibble extrusion, with as little as 0.44 log₁₀ reduction under optimized conditions. Formerly misclassified as Lactobacillus sporogenes; strain GBI-30, 6086 is the most commercially studied. In dogs, Acuff & Aldrich (2021) demonstrated maintained viability through the canine GIT with increased relative abundance of Bacillus genus in fecal microbiota at ≥10⁷ CFU/day. In cats, direct clinical trial data is limited, but AAFCO-listed status, GRAS recognition, and the lactic acid production mechanism support use. The spore-forming nature uniquely qualifies it as a practical probiotic in dry food — Weese & Arroyo (2003) found none of 19 commercial pet foods claiming probiotics contained all listed organisms, making spore-forming B. coagulans the most reliable choice for extruded kibble formats.
citations_display: Acuff HL, Aldrich CG. Effects of Bacillus coagulans GBI-30, 6086 probiotic on apparent nutrient digestibility, stool quality, intestinal health indicators, and fecal microbiota of healthy adult dogs. J Anim Sci. 2021;99(5):skab137. · Weese JS, Arroyo L. Bacteriological evaluation of dog and cat diets that claim to contain probiotics. Can Vet J. 2003;44(3):212-216.
position_context: This ingredient is beneficial regardless of position. Probiotics are added as precise supplements.

definition: H₃PO₄ functions as an acidifier, flavor enhancer, and emulsifier (FDA GRAS). Critically contributes highly bioavailable inorganic phosphorus with intestinal absorption of ~80–100%, substantially greater than organic phosphorus in meat/bone (~40–60%).
tldr: An acidifier and flavor enhancer that delivers highly bioavailable inorganic phosphorus — absorbed at ~80–100% versus ~40–60% for organic phosphorus in meat. Inappropriate for dogs or cats with kidney disease (IRIS Stage 2+). Especially significant for cats: CKD affects 30–40% of cats over 15, and phosphorus restriction is the most impactful dietary intervention for slowing progression. Recent evidence suggests excessive inorganic phosphorus may injure kidneys even in healthy cats.
detail_body: Phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄) functions as an acidifier, flavor enhancer, and emulsifier (FDA GRAS 21 CFR 182.1073). Critically, it contributes highly bioavailable inorganic phosphorus with intestinal absorption of ~80–100%, substantially greater than organic phosphorus bound in meat/bone (~40–60%). In dogs, healthy dogs manage moderate inorganic phosphorus loads via normal renal excretion, but dogs with CKD (IRIS Stage 2+) require phosphorus restriction with serum targets of 2.7–4.6 mg/dL (Stage 2) and <5.0 mg/dL (Stage 3). Foods containing phosphoric acid are inappropriate for canine CKD patients and warrant consideration for aging or at-risk breeds. In cats, this ingredient carries heightened clinical significance. CKD affects 30–40% of cats >15 years, and phosphorus restriction is the most impactful dietary intervention for slowing progression. IRIS 2023 targets: serum P <4.6 mg/dL (Stage 2), <5.0 mg/dL (Stage 3), <6.0 mg/dL (Stage 4). Recent evidence (Stockman, 2024) highlights that excessive soluble/inorganic phosphorus may induce kidney injury even in otherwise healthy cats. Phosphoric acid represents the most problematic form of dietary phosphorus for feline renal health.
citations_display: Stockman J. Dietary phosphorus and renal disease in cats: where are we? J Feline Med Surg. 2024;26(11). · International Renal Interest Society (IRIS). Treatment Recommendations for CKD in Cats. Updated 2023.
position_context: This ingredient's impact depends on how much is used. More phosphoric acid = more highly bioavailable inorganic phosphorus, which is directly relevant to CKD management.

definition: Lactic acid–producing bacterium (AAFCO-listed); strain SF68 (FortiFlora) is the most extensively studied veterinary probiotic with EFSA approval for dogs and cats.
tldr: The most clinically validated veterinary probiotic. Strain SF68 has strong evidence for reducing diarrhea in shelter cats (7.4% vs. 20.7% incidence in a double-blind trial) and stimulating immune function in young dogs. Important to distinguish E. faecium (established safety profile) from E. faecalis (higher pathogenic potential).
detail_body: Enterococcus faecium is a gram-positive, lactic acid–producing bacterium and AAFCO-listed direct-fed microorganism. Strain SF68 is the most extensively studied veterinary probiotic, with EFSA approval for dogs and cats. It is clinically important to distinguish E. faecium (established safety profile) from E. faecalis (higher pathogenic potential and nosocomial infection risk). In dogs, SF68 supplementation reduced fecal C. perfringens, increased beneficial bacteria, and stimulated immune function in young dogs (Benyacoub et al., 2003). In cats, a double-blinded shelter trial by Bybee et al. (2011) with 217 cats found SF68-treated cats had significantly fewer episodes of diarrhea lasting ≥2 days (7.4% vs. 20.7%, P=0.03) compared with placebo. Additional studies demonstrate maintained fecal microbiota diversity under stress.
